explanation.title: What is Python and Why Does It Matter?
explanation.content:
# What is Python?

Imagine you want to give a set of instructions to a very obedient helper. The helper will do exactly what you say, every time, without complaint — but only if you speak in a language it understands. **Python** is one of those languages, designed for giving precise instructions to a computer.

Python is a **programming language** — a formal set of words and rules that humans use to write programs. A **program** is simply a list of instructions the computer executes from top to bottom. Python was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. The name comes from the British comedy series *Monty Python's Flying Circus*, not from the reptile.

## Why Python Is Unique

Among the hundreds of programming languages that exist, Python is consistently ranked the most popular in the world. Several properties make it stand out:

- **Readable syntax** — Python code reads almost like plain English, so beginners can understand a program without memorising a long list of symbols
- **Versatility** — the same language powers web servers, machine learning models, automation scripts, games, and scientific research
- **Large standard library** — Python ships with thousands of pre-written tools so you do not have to build everything from scratch
- **Active community** — millions of developers have published free packages you can use immediately

## Where Python Is Used in the Real World

| Industry | What Python Does There |
|---|---|
| Artificial Intelligence | Training models behind ChatGPT and image recognition |
| Web Development | Running the backend of Instagram and Pinterest |
| Data Science | Analysing stock prices, medical records, and census data |
| Automation | Filling forms, renaming files, sending emails automatically |
| Education | Taught as the first language at MIT, IITs, and most CS programs |

## How Python Compares to Other Languages

| Language | Lines to print "Hello" | Main use case | Difficulty for beginners |
|---|---|---|---|
| Python | 1 | Everything | Very easy |
| Java | 5 | Enterprise software | Moderate |
| C++ | 4 | System software | Difficult |

Python's one-line "Hello, World!" — `print("Hello, World!")` — perfectly illustrates why beginners choose it first. In the code example below, you will see this simplicity in action alongside a few other fundamental `print()` calls.

codeExample.title: Python Introduces Itself
codeExample.code:
# Python executes instructions from top to bottom, one line at a time.
# Lines beginning with # are comments — Python ignores them completely.

# The print() function displays text on the screen.
print("Hello, World!")

# You can print any text inside the quotes.
print("Python was created in 1991.")
print("It is the most popular programming language today.")

# Python can also evaluate arithmetic and display the result.
print("Years since Python was created:", 2025 - 1991)

# Printing multiple items separated by commas adds a space between them.
print("Python", "is", "beginner", "friendly!")
codeExample.explanation:
- `# text` — a **comment**; Python skips this line entirely; use comments to explain your code to other humans
- `print("Hello, World!")` — calls the built-in `print()` function; whatever you pass inside the parentheses is displayed
- `"Hello, World!"` — a **string literal**; any characters surrounded by double quotes form a string
- `2025 - 1991` — Python evaluates arithmetic before printing; the screen shows `34`, not the expression
- `print("A", "B", "C")` — commas separate multiple items; Python inserts one space between each automatically

exercise.title: Write Your First Introduction Program
exercise.instructions:
Write a Python program that prints exactly four lines about yourself: your name on line 1, your city on line 2, your age as a number on line 3, and your favourite subject on line 4. Each line must use its own print() statement. A number does not need quotes; text does. Expected output example:
Priya Sharma
Mumbai
19
Mathematics
exercise.starterCode:
# My Introduction Program
# Each print() statement outputs one line.

# Line 1 — print your name
print("Your Name Here")

# Line 2 — print your city
print("Your City Here")

# Line 3 — print your age (a number, no quotes needed)
# Your code here

# Line 4 — print your favourite subject
# Your code here
exercise.solutionCode:
# My Introduction Program

print("Priya Sharma")
print("Mumbai")
print(19)
print("Mathematics")
hints: 💡 Each piece of information needs its own print() statement on a separate line.
💡 Text values like your name and city must go inside double quotes; a number like your age does not need quotes.
💡 Copy the pattern of the first two print() calls and write two more below them for age and subject.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes what Python is?
options: A hardware component that makes computers faster
A programming language used to write instructions for computers
A type of database for storing information
An operating system like Windows or macOS
questions.explanation: Python is a programming language — a formal set of rules and words that lets humans write instructions a computer can execute. It is not hardware, a database, or an operating system. After you write a Python program, the Python interpreter reads and runs those instructions.

questions.question: What does the # symbol do at the beginning of a line in Python?
options: It prints the rest of the line to the screen
It multiplies the value that follows it
It marks the line as a comment that Python ignores
questions.explanation: The # symbol starts a comment. Python skips everything on that line after the # character when the program runs. Comments are notes written by the programmer to explain the code to other humans. They have no effect on the program's output or behaviour.

questions.question: What will Python display when it runs print("Hello", "World")?
questions.explanation: When you separate multiple items with a comma inside print(), Python automatically places a single space between them. So print("Hello", "World") displays Hello World — no comma, just one space. The quotation marks are not displayed; they only mark where the string begins and ends.
